question.text: Open interest in derivative markets represents:

answer.text: The sum of all outstanding (incomplete) derivative contracts in the market
The total volume of contracts traded on a particular day
The number of new contracts created on a particular day
The interest earned on margin deposits with the clearing corporation

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Open interest is defined as the sum of all incomplete (outstanding) contracts in the market, represented as one-sided positions. These are positions that have not yet been closed, delivered, or expired. An increase in open interest indicates fresh money flowing into contracts, which is seen as a sign of liquidity. Higher open interest relative to traded volume indicates greater market liquidity.
**The Trap:** Open interest is NOT the same as trading volume — volume can be higher or lower than open interest on any given day. It is not about new contracts created on a single day. It has nothing to do with interest earned on margins.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Derivatives basics overlap with Series VIII, XIII. Open interest analysis is a key market indicator used by traders and is tested across all derivatives exams.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Open interest helps assess market sentiment and liquidity. Rising open interest alongside rising prices is considered bullish (fresh buying). Rising open interest with falling prices is considered bearish (fresh selling). This is a useful tool for F&O traders.
